summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – SECURITY FOR COSTS – Where the wife seeks to amend previous orders for security – Where leave is not required for substantive proceedings – Where property proceedings were finalised in a foreign court – Where the husband holds superannuation in Australia – Whether wife has complied with orders of foreign court to make to the husband – Contentions over assessment of quantum of security – Whether costs award in Australia would be enforceable in foreign court – Where previous orders are unchanged save as to period to make payment

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

GILL J

This judgment concerns the revisiting of an interlocutory order made on 24 September 2025 that stayed the applicant wife’s substantive application pending the payment into court of the sum of $20,000 as security for costs, in default of which the husband was entitled to seek the dismissal of the wife’s substantive application.
Despite being permitted 56 days to lodge the security, the wife has not, as at the time of this hearing, done so.
In response to the wife’s application to discharge the requirement for security, the husband seeks the dismissal of the substantive proceedings, but accepts that is a matter to be addressed following the resolution of the application.

The previous judgment

The judgment of 24 September 2025 sets out the general background circumstances that, subject to the qualifications expressed in this judgment, should be taken as adopted for the purposes of this judgment without being repeated.
In short form, these proceedings occur in a context of the parties living in, and having conducted the bulk of their relationship in Country B, where they have also resolved their property dispute (save for this dispute), and their parenting dispute. The application in this court is directed by the wife to a property adjustment of the superannuation interest held in Australia by the husband on the basis of contributions and s 75(2) (now s 79(5)) factors, but only for the period 8 October 2018 to 10 February 2020, a period of 16 months spanning the end of the relationship and predating the resolution of the Country B proceedings. That application to adjust is in the further context that the wife does not seek to re-open or re-litigate the Country B property division.

THE CURRENT APPLICATION

The wife seeks the discharge or amendment of the previous order for security. In support of such the wife identifies a number of complaints as to the prior judgment.
Of those, two matters, emphasised by three points in the conclusion to the wife’s outline of argument, have prominence. The first is the identification of the Full Court authority of Anderson & McIntosh [2013] FamCAFC 200 and the proposition contained therein that the time limit for the commencement of proceedings contained at s 44(3) of the Family Law Act 1975 has no application to a case such as this where the divorce took place in Country B. Accordingly, the assumption within the previous judgment that the wife would require leave, her application having been commenced more than 12 months following that divorce, was incorrect.
The second was that the conclusion that the wife had been non-compliant with a requirement imposed upon her by the District Court of Country B in the resolution of the property dispute to pay to the husband the sum of approximately Country B currency 17,000 was rendered incorrect or obsolete by the rejection of the husband’s attempt to enforce that debt. This formed a part of wider matters raised as to the issue of whether the wife would comply with an Australian order, or whether an Australian order would be enforceable against her in Country B.
The further key elements concerned the wife’s complaints about the assessment of the quantum of the security, and the potential that such will stifle her claim.

THE KEY ARGUMENTS

As identified in her summary of argument, the key issues raised by the wife are as to leave, the assessment of the enforcement risk, the assessment of quantum, and the prospect that the security requirement will prevent her from litigating, stifling the litigation.

Leave

As identified above, leave is not required.

The enforcement risk

The wife points to two factors, being the ability to register orders in Country B, and what she appeared to put forward as a demonstration that the description in the previous judgment that she had not met her obligation to pay the husband Country B currency 17,252.40 was now displaced by the husband’s failed attempt to enforce.
As to the first point, the wife makes the evidentially unsupported assertion that a recognition of, and enforcement of foreign judgments process is available in Country B. Perhaps this is so. It is not established that there is such a mechanism effective to enforce a costs award made in Australia.
As to the second point the wife asserts that there is no unpaid order, as the husband has not “filed an estate distributor’s determination with the Country B court to obtain an executable title – a step required to create an enforceable obligation.” She notes that the husband has not requested payment in over three years and previously asserted that there were no debts owing to him, despite then asserting that this remains unpaid. She observed that his attempt to enforce has, to date, been unsuccessful.
However, despite pointing to a failure to take adequate steps to enforce, in her oral submissions, when pressed on the point, the wife did not contend that there was no such outstanding order of the court, or that that she made a corresponding payment. Rather she pointed to the so far unsuccessful enforcement by the husband. She did not suggest that the rebuff he has currently encountered marks the end of enforcement of the order. The point previously made that the wife has not complied with the requirement to make the payment to the husband remains.

The costs quantum complaints

This second point involved a number of propositions. The first was a recanvassing of the financial matters raised in the previous hearing and a complaint that at the previous hearing the wife lacked the opportunity to address the husband’s costs contentions, as access to a subpoena addressed to the husband’s solicitors was delayed.
From the information provided on subpoena the wife submits that the balance actually held in the solicitor’s trust account is less than disclosed on the Costs Notice filed 23 September 2025. Exhibit W4 contains the subpoenaed material and is a trust account receipt recording a payment of $3,000 received on 21 July 2025.
However, the scope of the subpoena was directed to any payments made by the husband to his solicitor’s firm up to 1 September 2025 rather than to the date of the Costs Notice. On his Costs Notice dated 23 September 2025 the husband deposes to $11,000 being held on trust. It is not clear that any default in disclosure or production took place, or that such impacts materially upon the assessment.
The wife further complained that the quantum to dispose of the leave phase of the proceedings estimated by the husband of $30,000 was not adequately supported.
The wife observes that the solicitor did not provide an estimate to completion of the leave phase (submitted to be $30,000 at the last hearing). In the previous judgment I accepted that the amount submitted by the husband’s solicitor of $30,000 to complete the leave phase was a reasonable estimate. Within the context of the careful articulation by the husband’s solicitor, contained within his affidavit, of costs to completion, I remain of the view that this was a reasonable estimate had leave been required.
As noted in the previous hearing the husband only sought two thirds of that amount.
As observed by the husband, now that it is clear that there is no threshold phase of determining leave, the whole of the proceedings will be in play, meaning that the amount previously sought on the basis of leave will fall short of what may be incurred, estimated at about $69,000.
Again this is a reasonable observation. While the husband did not seek to increase the security amount, it may be accepted that an amount that was reasonable for a threshold issue of leave will leave the husband with diminished protection in respect of the substantive proceedings.
This point does not assist the wife.

THE OTHER CONTENTIONS

The other contentions raised by the wife, identified in her case summary document, may be summarised as follows:
(a) A repeating of the circumstances of the relationship and the Country B judgment;

(b) Complaint about the scope of the initial hearing, in particular in dealing with merits issues;

(c) Complaint as to threshold issues including;

(i) Summary dismissal (no summary dismissal order was made);

(ii) Estoppel (no determination of estoppel was made, rather such was identified as an issue albeit one that would fall upon the husband to establish);

(iii) Forum non-conveniens (no determination or rejection of forum was undertaken, merely that this is an issue to be pursued by the husband);

(iv) Abuse of process (no determination of abuse of process was made, merely that this is a potential issue given the resolution of property proceedings within Country B);

(d) The Country B appeal point;

(e) Spousal maintenance; and

(f) Criticism of the evidence of, and disclosure by, the husband in relation to costs.

The bulk of these matters were canvassed in the previous hearing and addressed in the previous judgment. Those other than the issues that are dealt with above, are further dealt with below.

Circumstances

The wife’s re-examination of the circumstances of the relationship appears to cover no new material ground from that pursued in the first hearing.

Merits

The wife complained that the previous hearing of the matter dealt with arguments as to, and assessment of the merits of her application. Assessment of merits forms a part of the consideration of the application for security. It formed a necessary part of the scope of the first hearing. The wife’s major complaint is the interplay between the consideration of merits and the mischaracterisation of the wife’s application as requiring leave. The significance of leave not being required will be dealt with further below.

Country B appeal point

The previous judgment noted that, although the wife contends that the Country B Court was in error in treating the husband’s Australian superannuation as analogous to pensions in Country B that are excluded assets for the purpose of property division, and although the wife prosecuted an appeal against aspects of the Country B Court’s decision, the asserted mis-characterisation of the husband’s superannuation was not pursued by the wife on that appeal.
The wife says that this is of no consequence as the Country B Court could not divide the Australian superannuation. Accepting that only an Australian court could split the superannuation, what has not been addressed is the capacity of the Country B Courts to otherwise take the amount into account. The issue is not merely one of whether the amount could be divided, but whether it could be taken into account, which the un-appealed aspect of the judgment determined it could not.
As identified in the previous judgment, this remains a potential Anshun estoppel issue, the burden of establishing of which falls upon the husband. The wife’s explanation advances her case no further.

Spousal maintenance issue

The wife says that she does not at present pursue her spousal maintenance claim. In the previous hearing the husband submitted that the reason that the wife disclosed for pursuing such indicated that her proceedings in Australia were not genuine. The wife asserts that as she is not pursuing such a claim at present that claim falls away.
Given that the previous judgment did not determine that the wife’s application was other than genuine, this is an arid submission by the wife.

Clarification of the wife’s case

By her outline of case the wife clarified that the relief she seeks against the superannuation is based on a discrete period, predating the Country B proceedings, of October 2018 to February 2020. She contends that
it is reasonably arguable that it is just and equitable for this Court to make a confined order addressing those discrete matters recognising: (i) the parties’ relative financial contributions and capacity to accumulate during the defined period; and (ii) the consequential disparity in their respective financial positions and retirement provision, including the absence of any offsetting adjustment of the respondent’s excluded Australian superannuation interest... .
It is difficult to accept that such a novel and fragmented approach to s 79 proceedings could be reasonably arguable.

DISCUSSION

The wife correctly identifies that she does not need to obtain leave. This removes a potentially significant hurdle that the previous judgment considered would be difficult for the wife to cross. This is a matter that is supportive of the wife’s application to change the order for security.
The other matters raised by the wife do not materially advantage her beyond what was considered in the previous judgment.
Accepting that security for costs is an exceptional remedy, and that the amount imposed may stifle the wife’s claim, there remain strong reasons to maintain the orders.
Those reasons relate to significant issues with the wife’s prospects for success in her application. The combination of the litigation of the matter in Country B, within which consideration was given to the superannuation interest in Australia, and it was determined to be, like the pension interest of the wife in Country B, an excluded asset, with the wife’s proposed novel approach to property adjustment raise, at face value, deep doubt as to the wife’s application holding any merit.
The reasonable potential for issues of forum, estoppel, and abuse of process to be raised do not improve that position.
In that context, the same issues as to enforcement remain as identified in the previous judgment. The wife’s contentions as to the outstanding obligation did not address the substance of the position that she has still not made good on the obligation cast upon her to make payment to the husband in Country B, illustrating the need for security for costs where the application by the wife appears tenuous.
As to quantum, while the wife may struggle to make the payment, it is not clear that she will be unable to. Further, the quantum established now falls well short of what would be reasonably protective of the husband’s costs of defending the claim.

CONCLUSION

No change should be made to the previous orders, other than to provide the wife with further reasonable time for compliance with the payment of $20,000 to the court for security of costs.
A reasonable period of 28 days will be allowed for making the payment of security.
Directions will be given on delivery of judgment for the husband’s application for dismissal of the wife’s claim in the event she does not pay the security within 28 days.
